macro_segments.title: The Debate Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yochanan: Whether the Borrower Must Return Ribbis D'Oraisa

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Dispute: Ribbis D'Oraisa as Distinguished from Gezel (Theft)
Mishnah from Bava Kamma: Children Not Obligated to Return Ribbis; Inference About the Father
Gemara's Clarification: The Mishnah Discusses Only Cases Where Children Must Return (Specific Objects)
Question on the Mishnah: Why Protect the Honor of a Father Who Lent with Ribbis?
Resolution: The Father Performed Teshuvah Before Death

macro_segments.title: Baraita: Thieves and Ribbis Lenders Must Return; Analysis of Their Transgression

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Gazlanim and Malvei Ribbis Must Return Even After Collection
Gemara's Parsing: What Does 'Thieves' Mean for Those Who Lend with Ribbis?
Dispute Among Tannaim: Rav Nechemia and Rabbi Eliezer Ben Yaakov vs. Implicit First Tanna
Clarification: Qum Aseh Refers to Tearing Up the Shettar, Not Collecting Ribbis
Mishnah: Those Who Transgress in Ribbis—Lender, Borrower, Guarantor, and Witnesses

macro_segments.title: Determining Ribbis D'Oraisa vs. Ribbis D'Rabbanan: The Test of Secular Enforcement

micro_segments.title: Rav Safra's Principle: Ribbis D'Oraisa Is What Would Be Enforced in Non-Jewish Courts
Challenge from Abaye: Sa'ah B'Sa'ah (Measure for Measure) as Counterexample
Sa'ah B'Sa'ah Explained: Borrowing and Returning Equal Quantities of Fungible Goods
Rav Safra's Response: Sa'ah B'Sa'ah Treated as Pikadon (Deposit), Not Ribbis
Mashkanta B'Lo Nechiyata (Mortgage Without Deduction): Another Counterexample
Rav Safra's Response: Mashkanta Treated as Liens Giving Partial Ownership, Not Ribbis
Refined Principle: Ribbis Ketzutza (Fixed Interest) Matches Secular Enforcement; All Other Cases Exempt
Ribbis Mukdemet and Ribbis Me'ucheret: Early and Late Interest as Non-Contractual Ribbis D'Rabbanan

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Buying Grain at Harvest, Reselling at Higher Price, and Converting Debt to Wine

micro_segments.title: Case Setup: Agreeing to Buy Grain at Harvest Price for Later Delivery
Leniency: Once Market Price Is Set, Seller Can Deliver Later Even Without Possession
Problem Case: Converting Debt to Wine (Not Money) When Seller Lacks Wine
Rava's Explanation: The Mishnah Involves a Debt Without Money Changing Hands

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Ha'aramas Ribbis: Permitted Acts Forbidden Due to Appearance of Ribbis

micro_segments.title: Rav Safra's Teaching: Some Things Permitted in Principle but Forbidden as Ha'aramas Ribbis

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Application to the Mishnah: Reinterpreting the Grain-Wine Case as Ha'aramas Ribbis

micro_segments.title: Abaye Proposes: The Mishnah Case Exemplifies Ha'aramas Ribbis From Rav Safra's Teaching

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Application to the Mishnah: Reinterpreting the Grain-Wine Case as Ha'aramas Ribbis (continued)

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Scenario: Selling Grain for Thirty, Buying Back for Twenty-Five, Then Reselling at Higher
Result: Net Effect Is Twenty-Five Given, Thirty Returned (Apparent Interest)

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Ha'aramas Ribbis: Permitted Acts Forbidden Due to Appearance of Ribbis (continued)

micro_segments.title: Baraita Example: Lending Wheat, Then Buying It Back for Less Money
Analysis: Whether Lending or Selling; Result Is Twenty-Five Given, Twenty-Five Owed
Permission If Paid Back in Produce (Wine); Prohibition If Paid Back in Cash

macro_segments.title: Rava's Objection: Abaye's Scenario Does Not Match the Mishnah's Language

micro_segments.title: Rava Points Out: Mishnah Says 'Give Me Grain' Not 'Give Me Money'; Doesn't Say 'Sold to Me'
Rava Also Notes: Mishnah Says Debt Becomes Thirty, Not That It Was Thirty Originally
Rava's Conclusion: Abaye's Emendations Cannot Rescue His Reading; Pshat Remains Unsolved

Ribbis Mukdemet and Ribbis Me'ucheret (Early and Late Interest)

Rav Pinchas in name of Rava
